sentences.text: He gave us advice as how to do it.
You know, he learned us how to do it.
He had experience in this.
And said it should be easier to work.
He took-- I'll pay you with-- with-- with the soap water and add a little bit of water.
It should be-- should go slide through it and then put all the bodies on top of it.

And then one picks up from the back, and two guys-- two guys by the first stick, two guys by the other stick-- with that it was five people picked up the five-- the-- the three bodies, put them on the rails.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the sixth one had a fork, which attached to the back of the-- of the body.
And when the rail was inside in the oven, he held back this here, and they moved out-- out to this-- this-- this-- this fixture.
And this was 15 bodies.
It took 20 minutes-- 15 ovens, 3 bodies, 20 minutes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Wholesale.
My shift was [NON-ENGLISH].
Oh, my shift?
My shift changed.
Once, night shift.
Once, day shift.
That's how it was.
Night shift and day shift.
Night shift-- so it wasn't I'm night, all the-- all the time, night.
One day, one night.
And that's how it was.
But-- but-- but we were one kommando.
There were-- there were more than one kommando.
There were many kommandos, many kapos.
But shifts, each-- each-- each kommando from one kapo had two shifts, night shift and day shift.
To the block.
No.
No.
Our block was-- our block was blocked.
There was a-- there was a-- a-- what do you call it?
A guard.
Not a SS guard, but from the block, a guard.
And the-- the blockalteste was-- was responsible for it.
George, Sergio-- Sergio-- Serge.
Serge was his name, from the sonderkommando.
Maybe you heard about it.
I remember the last name?
Serge, he was a French.
He was French.
I know-- I know exactly how his face looked.
But he was-- what was his name?
He was-- maybe I'll-- I'll recall.
August Bruck was the oberkapo.
German.
He was German.
He wasn't the-- he was sent from the-- from-- from the political-- this here.
He was a quiet man.
He looked-- he looked like like a settled man.
He was a criminal.
He must have been a criminal.
But he didn't treat us bad.
He just wanted the work.
He wanted the work.
And he was just like some, with-- with-- with the political.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
No.
If we had our shift, we serve the whole crematorium.
Our shift, then came another shift, and took over the same thing.
So we were in block two, crematoria two.
Block two, crematoria two.
Not all the time.
No, no way.
No way.
No way.
No way.
Because they build, now, there, then-- they built-- they built-- they kept building and building and building, blocks and blocks and blocks and blocks.
This was from the old.
And then they took us into-- to camp D, block 13.
Block 13 was sonderkommando.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But this wasn't at the time.
So the first transport to this-- when-- when the first transport came to block-- to sonderkommando two, they build us a sonderkommando three, one opposite the other.
Two and three, opposite the other, they built.
But the first one, it was in-- in March, '93.
They brought the first-- they-- they made-- they made the-- the-- the renewing, the renewing from Cracow.
A shipment from Cracow.
And this was the number 108.
I am 93.
The first big transferred from Cracow.
And this crematorium was built when you-- when they brought in the trucks, it was a pitch going down, with-- with a-- like-- like, a way going down, and facing a basement.
But this was a basement, it was big, to take in a few thousand people.
You know?
And from there was the-- this was the-- this was the dressing room with-- with numbers, with the hook, this and this, this and this.
And then there's a shower room.
And next thing was a big door going to-- to the shower room.
And this was the gas chamber.
But all-- all the signs were shower room, dressing room, shower, this and that.
And then it was a big elevator.
A big elevator, taking-- taking in 10-- 10-- 10 bodies like-- like nothing.
Up and down.
So this-- the first was the Cracow transport.
Then I was there for a while.
And they built-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] little by little they took over the kommando, the sonderkommando which were by the bunkers.
Most probably what I heard is it didn't work out, by the bunkers.
It didn't work out.
It didn't do the job.
And they had to go back to the job because-- because of the smell.
And of the summer-- the-- the-- the bodies smell.
You know, it was oozing.
It couldn't-- it couldn't-- it couldn't take-- it couldn't take this work.
So they-- they had-- they-- they-- they employed the people there, to open up and burn them.
In the beginning they-- they-- they-- they buried them.
And then they burned them.
Because the whole thing they had to open up and-- and-- the whole thing and-- and burn them again, and start burning them.
But later on, this kommando was part of the-- was the sonderkommando.
We were only a few.
And they learned the trade too.
They learned the trade.
I mean, the-- [NON-ENGLISH].
The [NON-ENGLISH].
The [NON-ENGLISH] were the-- the first ones which they were-- schleppers, there was a lot.
You know what it mean, schleppers?
Huh?
Schleppers are all you had to do are-- is a belt from-- from-- from the pants.
And put them on the hand.
And you schlep.
All by the feet.
This is the way that they-- they did it.
You know?
And-- and to-- they pulled-- they had to pour water, it should be slippery.
And that was before they brought them in.
They had to take him to cut the hair, and cut-- take out the teeth.
And then they bring him there.
And then they-- the [NON-ENGLISH].
So they built, also, four and five.
On the same-- where one-- two and three was, a little bit further up, near the forest was a little [INAUDIBLE].
They built another two.
And I was assigned to the four and five.
Four and five.
And there, there was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was two kapos.
One was Leizer kapo.
And one was Schlomo, Schlomo kapo.
I was with Leizer.
He was-- he was a-- a [NON-ENGLISH].
No, Bialystok, there.
[NON-ENGLISH].
Then-- then at five.
Then at five.
Beginning at four, then at five.
Coming there-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they were-- we worked there.
We had steady, steady supply.
There was one guy-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the SS scharfuhrer, Quackernack.
He was-- we called him the [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what it means, [NON-ENGLISH]?
Because he always came with [INAUDIBLE].
He's-- a shipment is coming.
A shipment is coming.
He was-- he-- he-- he looked like-- like-- he looked like-- like an ape.
You know?
With his face, and tooth.
But tough.
And the kommando fuhrer, scharfuhrer was-- was-- his name was--
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Can't remember.
Foss.
F-O-S-S.
Oberscharfuhrer Foss.
He was the kommando-- he was the kommando fuhrer from this crematorium.
In-- in our-- and there, well, there were SS also.
The SS-- the SS was some Germans.
And some were Ukraines.
And some were Lithuanians, Lithuanians.
So you work there, and it was busy.
Busy from-- from-- from-- from-- France is France.
Belgium is Belgium.
And Thessaloniki is-- is Greece.
And-- and then from Crete.
You've got from all over, coming.
You know?
And Holland.
Holland is-- every day.
Every day.
And this was going on-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] for a long time.
We-- but when it came small things, small things, so they brought in with a truck, whatever.
If it was 100, it didn't pay.
You know, to-- to-- there were-- there were some-- three-- three guest rooms.
And for small amounts, it didn't pay.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- but the kapo, Leizer kapo, or whatever, he-- he was one of us.
And he warned us, sometimes, you know, to-- if we try to, a little bit, goof around, and he some-- he sometimes came with the bigger-- a bigger echelon came there to-- to look around, to check it.
But generally, he handled him.
And I guess he bribed him too.
Because by the transports, by the shipments, when they finished, when they undressed-- the kommando undressed, we were by the ovens.
And he was the kapo.
And after the undressing, they-- they-- they chased him to the-- to the gas chambers.
They closed the door.
The knock-- they closed the door and you could hear the knockings, knockings, knockings.
You know?
They were fooled around.
They-- they were fooled into-- into-- to go there.
They saw signs to the bath, to the bath.
And all of a sudden, they locked the door.
And they closed it, permanent.
And then on-- on top of the roof there was a chimney with a steel guard, which it should-- it goes-- going down, to-- to the bunker.
And they poured in the gas over there, the Zyklon.
And this evaporated.
But it-- this-- this-- this, on heat, it makes a smoke.
And the smoke, the smoke, it just-- it kills.
So you heard banging on the doors.
You heard screaming in the beginning.
But after a while, you heard less and less and less, till-- till-- so after that, the schleppers, whatever, and the kommando, they had to put on gas masks to open the doors.
They-- they were in the back doors, windows, and-- and-- and air it out.
And they had to put on gas masks to pull out the people from there.
The people were soiled.
And the people were-- were also bent, one to another, families.
You know?
A father with a son with a wife, like this.
You know, the-- the kids, young ones.
The strong ones-- the strong ones, always, run on the top.
Because they try to go to the door.
But everybody try to go to the door.
And away from-- from the-- from the guard.
Because the guard, they-- they-- they saw, coming out, this-- this-- this gas.
But it didn't help anything.
It didn't-- it didn't help anything.
After 10-- for 10 minutes, you didn't hear a sound.
So they cleaned up.
Brought it in there.
And this was the work.
Pushing in, picking up the-- the-- the board on the rails.
One of those, or with the guard holding back and pulling back.
This was the work.
Survive?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a lot to say.
Because-- because it is only through pure luck that I'm here.
If I wouldn't be here, there wouldn't be a storyteller.
There was many, many, many times that I supposed to be on the selection.
Because they-- they-- from time to time, they made selection, and they took a few hundred down.
They send them and they send them to-- to-- to Lublin.
You know?
They say, to Lublin, there, and here, you know, [NON-ENGLISH].
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we didn't hear from them.

But sometimes-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when from Lublin, Russians came to Auschwitz-- to-- to Birkenau, some of them recognized that they wear boots and-- and-- and garments from the people who went there.
Because they send them over here.
When-- when the Russians were near.
So-- and so they told the story.
They-- a-- a secret cannot be forever.
So this is how it was.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on the point with-- with-- with food, somehow we had a handle on it.
You know what I mean?
Because after they went there, they left everything behind.
And-- and the kommando had to-- to-- to lift everything to the-- to the truck.
They-- they had-- there was a kommando.
It's name, Kanada.
And this Kanada took in everything which-- which the people brought in.
They-- they handled it later.
But the food, if somebody had had a handle on it, they tried to-- to-- to have some food.
So this was the way-- this was the way sonderkommando had-- had-- had a chance to eat more than the other ones.
And that's what-- that's-- this was the story.
Was there black marketing?
Were people able to approach you at all?
Black market?
Black--
Well, I can tell you this.
There are some of them, there, are here.
They have connections.
I had no connections.
I had no connections.
And they were organize-- they-- they say-- we heard-- we heard in there that something is coming up.
In the air is something, a revolution, something, a upstanding.
You know what I mean?
With the-- with the whole camp.
And once when I was in-- on-- on-- on-- on-- on-- on-- on day shift, and the night shift left, I heard that it's supposed to be-- at--at that night it's supposed to be something coming up.
All of a sudden, the-- the kommando, the-- the-- the-- the Russians, they didn't-- the Russians or the Polacks, they didn't rush.
Because it-- it didn't-- they were not as hot as-- as the sonderkommando were hot.
You know what I mean?
In the sonderkommando it was only one way.
You come in.
But you cannot go out.
You cannot wait of-- you cannot wait for-- for-- for miracles.
If it-- if they had, I-- for example, in middle of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] of now, of '43, my six months being there, they had this-- this clearing up from Sosnowiec, [NON-ENGLISH].
This-- this was day and night.
This went for day and night there.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what I want to say?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So we were-- so and afterward, with the slack, they took out a few hundred.
So before it came, they-- they put in a few hundred people.
The few hundred people were the most engaged in the gas chambers, bringing into the to the-- to the-- the place where-- what do they call it?
A-- a-- a-- a mortuary.
The mortuary.
And from there, they're brought into the crematorium.
So those, they changed back and forth.
You know what I mean?
As soon as got slack, they took out 200 people.
They send them out to-- to Lublin, to-- to [NON-ENGLISH].
To-- they-- they send them.
Yeah, we have a job.
We have plenty jobs.
But actually, this was the end.
So the sonderkommando was more in it than-- than the lager.
Always, always, when it came a-- when it came time to-- to-- to act, they said, wait a minute.
We have a little time.
And then the Russians started moving.
And they said, well, we're going to-- we-- we can wait.
But we couldn't wait.
After that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it dragged to '43.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One time came a little-- a little shipment.
So it was the families, mothers and kids.
This mother came in.
She had a little baby.
And the Foss-- Foss, the Oberscharfuhrer Foss, he was in-- he was the kommando fuhrer.
But when it came to small things, he had-- he had the-- the rifle a-- a-- a-- a silencer.
So this lady comes in with the kid.
And he goes in with this rifle.
And she turns back-- he goes to the kid.
And she turns back to-- with him and cover-- covers the kid.
And goes here.
She covers around.
All of a sudden, he gives a bang in-- in-- in the kids.
So the mother takes the kid and throws him in his face.
He was bloody from-- from head to toe.
And he says to-- to-- to this girl-- this-- this-- he was the underscharfuhrer, under him.
[NON-ENGLISH].
You know what it means?
[NON-ENGLISH].
Handicap.
He gave it to-- he gave there, this.
He gave him the-- he was bloody, this-- this was-- this was-- this was stories which-- which you can tell and tell.
Then once they brought in [SIGHS] a small transport to-- a-- a-- a transport.
There came SS with-- with the-- with the-- what do you call it?
The-- those-- [NON-ENGLISH] You know what it means, the hats?
The-- the black-- black-- it was-- Like a beret?
Not the beret.
No, no, hats.
But-- but with-- with-- with the black corduroy, you know, around to this here, but officers.
What his name?
Kitt.
Kitt.
One was Kitt.
The other one was another name.
These were doctors.
Doctors.
So he-- he selected from the transport.
He selected, say, 20 young, fresh.
Well, they just selected them.
They must be good.
And the rest of it, they send them to-- to-- to the gas chamber.
Regular work.
Those 20 had [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH], special-- special treatment, shooting.
Not gassing, shooting.
And then they try to work on them.
They cut the pieces.
Cut pieces from-- from-- from their thighs.
They cut pieces from the-- from the-- from this here-- muscles, and in-- and in pails.
And when they cut those pieces, the pieces were still alive.
You know?
They were-- they were moving.
But this was the work there.
The doctors, this was-- this was special doctors, do it.
No?
Didn't-- And they did that inside where you were?
Inside the--
In the mortuary.
--mortuary.
In the mortuary.
We were by the ovens.
This-- this crematorium four was in a smaller form than the crematorium two and three.
Four and five was in that smaller form than two and three.
Two and three were 15 ovens.
And here were only eight.
So and-- and the mortuary was next to it.
In the middle was-- was a dentist and a hair cutter.
You know?
It was a little room.
And a hair cutter.
And then came every-- every here and there they came-- came out to collect the teeth.
You know?
And after the mortuary were the bunkers, the gas chambers.
So it wasn't so big.
We could-- we could-- we could see through.
Yeah.
I told you, Kitt, and the other one I got written down.
And the other, written down.
Dentist, Jewish dentists?
Jewish dentists?
They took in to-- two Jewish dentists, French.
They were-- they-- from a transport, they asked for dentists.
And they thought they're going to have dentists.
You know what I mean?
Because there-- there was also a-- a-- a-- what do you call it?
A-- a-- a-- a recovery room, whatever, they had, for-- for sick people.
And meanwhile, they took him into-- to-- to-- to-- to the crematorium to be the dentist, not to pull teeth.
Pull teeth was a-- was a prisoner, like-- like us, a regular one, a prisoner.
Oh yeah, yeah, his name was Epstein.
But he-- he did things-- we did-- we did the-- the-- the-- the ovens.
He did the pulled-- the-- the teeth pulling.
And one that did the hair cutting.
As a matter of fact, the hair cutting wasn't my kommando.
But in another kommando was Kaminski.
You heard about Kaminski?
No.
He was-- he was also a nice-- a-- a Jewish kapo.
And he had crematorium two, three.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
And he-- he had-- in his commando he had [NON-ENGLISH], you know?
A whole selection of those people.
So he gave them the hair selection, a special job to collect the hair.
You know?
Because they-- they-- he didn't want them to-- to-- to work on the other things.
So where was I then, there?
We were talking about the doctors and the--
Yeah.
The dentists.
And Kaminski took these group of Hasidic young men?
Yeah. yeah.
And he put him up for the special job there.
But the dentist, by the end of it I'll-- I'll come-- I have to jump from one thing to the other.
And then come back.
The end of the dentist was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that when the [NON-ENGLISH] was.
There was an [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what it means, [NON-ENGLISH].
Our-- our world.
They took their lives.
They took their own lives.
Because they-- they had-- they kept him there.
There was no way out.
If you smelled sonderkommando, this was the end of it.
You couldn't go out of there.
So what-- they took-- they took-- they took their own-- their own-- their own lives.
They-- they fell into sonderkommando, as dentists.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
And then the-- what the dentist did, they melt-- they melt the teeth what this Epstein pulled out, who was this-- this-- this-- pull-- pull-- he was a tooth puller.
And took out-- he put it in a-- in a basket.
The SS came and took this basket.
And they gave it to them.
And they melted it.
And they returned it to-- to-- to the-- to the-- German [INAUDIBLE].
You you know what I mean?
It's gold.
It's not only life, but we can use gold too.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
From-- from--
From the jobs?
They-- we-- we were all one kommando, sonderkommando.
Kaminski was kapo.
Was a kapo in-- in crematorium two and three.
We had a kapo, Leizer.
And there were Schlomo, which we [INAUDIBLE].
And then--
OK.
